module_schema.learning_objectives: 3-5 measurable objectives per module, prefixed with action verbs (analyze, distinguish, evaluate, trace, synthesize)
module_schema.key_terms: Bolded on first use, defined in context, collected in a Key Terms subsection
module_schema.gsd_through_line: Each module ends with a 'GSD Ecosystem Connection' section mapping concepts to gsd-skill-creator architecture
module_schema.cross_module_connections: Explicit forward/backward references to other modules using [Module N: Title] format
module_schema.assessment_questions: 3-5 questions per module that test understanding, not memorization
module_schema.sources: All citations use [Author, Year] format with full entry in Sources section. SC-03: every statistical claim has named source + sample size.

citation_template.full_entry: Author(s). (Year). Title. Publisher/Journal, Volume(Issue), Pages. URL (if applicable)

safety_rules.SC-01: Non-Western traditions: use nation/culture-specific attribution. NEVER 'Eastern science' or generic groupings.
safety_rules.SC-02: AI-generated hypotheses: always qualify as AI-generated, never present as validated scientific findings without explicit qualification.
safety_rules.SC-03: Statistical claims: every percentage, replication rate, survey figure must have named source with sample size.
safety_rules.SC-04: Indigenous knowledge: no content without nation-specific OCAP consultation. Defer to dedicated mission.

primary_philosophical.entry: Popper, K.R. (1959). The Logic of Scientific Discovery. Hutchinson, London.
Kuhn, T.S. (1962). The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. University of Chicago Press.
Feyerabend, P. (1975). Against Method. New Left Books, London.
Lakatos, I. (1970). Falsification and the methodology of scientific research programmes. In Criticism and the Growth of Knowledge. Cambridge University Press.
Bernard, C. (1865). An Introduction to the Study of Experimental Medicine. Paris.
Mill, J.S. (1843). A System of Logic. London.

encyclopedias.entry: Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy: Scientific Method. plato.stanford.edu/entries/scientific-method/
Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy: Karl Popper. iep.utm.edu/pop-sci/

peer_reviewed.entry: Open Science Collaboration (2015). Estimating the reproducibility of psychological science. Science, 349(6251).
Baker, M. (2016). 1,500 scientists lift the lid on reproducibility. Nature, 533, 452-454.
Ludwig, J., & Mullainathan, S. (2024). Machine learning as a tool for hypothesis generation. The Quarterly Journal of Economics, 139(2), 751-827.
O'Brien, T., et al. (2024). Machine learning for hypothesis generation in biology and medicine: FieldSHIFT. Digital Discovery, RSC Publishing.
PMC (2021). The Scientific Method: A Need for Something Better? pmc.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7965632/
Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence (2025). AI, agentic models and lab automation for scientific discovery. frontiersin.org
Munafo, M.R., et al. (2017). A manifesto for reproducible science. Nature Human Behaviour, 1(1), 0021.
